name: Province Lake
short_description: Scenic lakeside rockhounding area in the White Mountains region offering opportunities to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens in glacially-sorted sediments and bedrock outcrops.
description:
Province Lake sits in the foothills of New Hampshire's White Mountains at a modest 487 feet elevation with gentle slopes that make it accessible for rockhounders of varying skill levels. This area lies within the New England province where Precambrian metamorphic bedrock underlies glacial deposits rich in mineral-bearing stones. The lake itself was carved by glacial activity during the Pleistocene, and the surrounding terrain contains exposed bedrock and sorted glacial materials that yield quartz crystals, feldspar chunks, and mica flakes.

The region's geology is dominated by metamorphosed granite and schist formations typical of the White Mountains. Glacial retreat left behind excellent sorting of mineral fragments, making this an ideal location for finding clean specimens of common New England minerals. Rockhounders typically work along shorelines, stream beds, and gentle hillsides where weathering and water action have concentrated lighter minerals.

Access is relatively straightforward with passenger vehicle parking available near the lake. The gentle slope and low elevation mean collecting can be comfortable during the shoulder seasons. Beginners should focus on exposed banks and wash areas where minerals are naturally concentrated. The combination of glacial deposits and nearby bedrock outcrops provides diverse collecting opportunities without requiring technical equipment or permits.
history: Province Lake owes its current landscape to Pleistocene glaciation that scoured the bedrock and deposited sorted mineral-rich glacial sediments across the region. The underlying bedrock consists of Precambrian metamorphic granite and schist formations that were heavily weathered and fractured by glacial action, exposing fresh mineral faces and creating excellent conditions for natural sorting and concentration of quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens.
tips: Search along the lake shoreline and small stream banks where water action has naturally concentrated lighter minerals like mica and quartz. Look for small quartz clusters and feldspar fragments in the glacial till on hillslopes; mica sheets often appear as shiny flakes in sandy sediment. Bring a small hand lens to identify feldspar color variations and mica cleavage. After rain, freshly exposed banks often yield the best specimens.
terrain_notes: Elevation 487 feet with gentle slopes throughout the collecting area. Terrain is a mix of glacial soil, sandy deposits, and exposed bedrock outcrops. Vegetation is typical New England forest with deciduous and mixed evergreen growth. Walking distances from parking to prime collecting areas range from a few hundred feet to half a mile. Footing is generally stable with minor rocky areas; comfortable sneakers are adequate.
best_season: May - September. These months offer the most pleasant weather for lakeside rockhounding, with warm temperatures and lower water levels exposing more shoreline material. Spring snowmelt in late April can churn up fresh specimens.
written_directions: From Effingham, take Route 153 north toward Province Lake for approximately 3 miles. Look for the Province Lake area access points on the right side of the road. Public parking areas are available near the lake. The main collecting areas are accessible via short walks from the parking pullouts along the lake perimeter.
road_conditions: Route 153 is paved highway in good condition. Access roads to lake parking are unpaved but passable by passenger vehicles in most seasons.
parking_notes: Multiple pullout parking areas around Province Lake. Capacity varies by location, typically 2-5 vehicles per pullout. No formal fee.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Respect private property boundaries around the lake. Do not trespass on private land. Collect only loose material and surface specimens. Do not excavate or remove bedrock. Follow Leave No Trace principles.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Respect private property boundaries
Collect loose surface material only
No excavation or bedrock removal
Follow Leave No Trace principles
nearest_services: Effingham, approximately 3 miles south, has basic services including gas and small stores. Larger services available in Conway, approximately 15 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Gentle slopes and easy terrain make this accessible to most hikers and families. Parking pullouts are at lake level with short walks to collecting areas. No wheelchair accessibility to collecting sites due to uneven terrain, but viewing areas may be accessible from parking. Sturdy walking shoes recommended.

faq.question: What minerals can I find rockhounding at Province Lake in New Hampshire?
faq.answer: Province Lake is known for quartz crystals, feldspar fragments, and mica flakes. These minerals occur naturally in the glacial deposits and exposed bedrock around the lake. Quartz specimens range from small crystals to larger chunks, while feldspar appears as milky or pinkish fragments. Mica sheets are commonly found in sandy shoreline areas.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Province Lake, New Hampshire?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Province Lake. However, be aware of and respect private property boundaries around the lake, as some shoreline areas are privately owned. Stick to public access points and parking areas.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Effingham?
faq.answer: May through September offers the best rockhounding conditions at Province Lake. Warm weather and lower water levels expose more shoreline material for collecting. Late spring after snowmelt can churn up fresh specimens. Avoid winter when snow and ice cover the ground and water levels are higher.

faq.question: Is Province Lake rockhounding suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Province Lake is excellent for beginners and families. The gentle slopes, easy terrain, and close parking make it accessible for children and all skill levels. Surface specimens are plentiful along the shoreline and in glacial deposits, so no specialized equipment or excavation is necessary.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Province Lake?
faq.answer: Basic tools include a hand lens for identifying minerals, a small digging tool for loose material, and a collection bag. Comfortable walking shoes are essential. Bring water and sunscreen. A field guide to New England minerals helps identify specimens. No heavy excavation equipment is needed or permitted.

faq.question: How far is Province Lake from major towns and what services are available?
faq.answer: Province Lake is located about 3 miles north of Effingham, which has gas stations and small stores. Conway, approximately 15 miles south, offers more comprehensive services including restaurants and shops. Cell service in the area is spotty, so plan accordingly before heading out.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Province Lake near Effingham, NH. Easy public access rockhounding in the White Mountains. Beginner-friendly.
